Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd tell myself I was going to study, but then I'd end up watching videos or scrolling through stuff without really focusing. I have all these resources available to me, so I'd download materials thinking I'd get to them later. Most weeks I'd put in maybe 18 hours, but a lot of that was just having things open rather than actually grinding through content.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I didn't have set study times—I'd just kind of drift into it when I felt like it or when something was due soon. Deadlines would make me scramble, but otherwise I just went with whatever felt right in the moment. I know consistency would help, but I never really locked that down.
Q3: Usually I'd log in because I got a notification about something or because I remembered an assignment was coming up. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if anything new was posted, kind of out of habit. It wasn't like I had this burning desire to learn—it was more like checking social media, just seeing what's there.
Q4: Yeah, logging in did become kind of automatic after a while. I'd do it between other things, like while eating or during breaks from my extracurriculars. It wasn't deep engagement though—more like a quick check-in. I got comfortable with the interface, so it became easy to just pop in and out.
Q5: The videos were definitely the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I could watch them at my own pace, pause, rewind if I zoned out. I watched a lot of video content actually—over 140 minutes worth. Diagrams and visual explanations stuck with me way better than reading dense text.
Q6: The discussion forums and reading materials, I barely touched those. Reading long articles felt tedious and I'd lose focus quickly. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later but later never really came. The peer interactions I did were minimal—I'd comment here and there but nothing substantial.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it was visually engaging. If something was going to take forever and I couldn't see how it connected to the exam, I'd skip it. Quick wins kept me interested; anything that felt like a slog got pushed aside.
Q8: Optional stuff felt like it didn't matter, honestly. If it wasn't required, my brain just categorized it as 'maybe later' which meant never. Required activities at least had some urgency attached. I know that's not the best mindset, but when you're juggling things, optional becomes invisible.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download PDFs and videos to my laptop and study from there without being connected to the LMS. I have good resources at home, so I'd use my tablet or laptop offline a lot. Sometimes I'd pull up YouTube videos that explained concepts better than the course materials.
Q10: There were times I'd be reviewing downloaded notes or rewatching saved videos while lying in bed or during commutes. None of that showed up anywhere. I'd also talk through concepts with friends outside of the official discussion boards. The LMS only saw a fraction of what I was doing.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a video once, download it, and then watch it multiple times offline. Same with lecture slides—I'd grab them and study from my downloads folder. The LMS would just show one access, but I might have reviewed that material three or four times on my own.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. I started feeling scared about where I was headed academically. My attendance was basically non-existent and I knew my exam scores weren't great. That fear kind of paralyzed me instead of motivating me—I'd avoid the course because facing it felt overwhelming.
Q13: My extracurriculars took up a lot of mental space, and sometimes I'd be too tired to really engage with coursework. When I was confused about material, I'd just skip it rather than push through. The workload from my four online courses felt manageable on paper but overwhelming in practice.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd have videos playing but I'd be thinking about other stuff or half-watching while doing something else. I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. It looked like engagement but my brain was somewhere else entirely.
Q15: I postponed exam prep constantly even though I knew it was crucial. The assignments I could handle—got through most of them—but actual studying for exams felt daunting. I think I was scared of finding out how much I didn't know, so I just kept putting it off until it was too late.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd see something was due and suddenly I'd be active on the LMS, downloading stuff, submitting work. Without that external pressure, I'd drift. My task completion was decent because deadlines forced action, but the quality suffered.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. By the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally. If it was positive, cool. If it was negative, I'd feel bad for a minute but then just continue doing what I was doing. I didn't use it to adjust my approach like I probably should have.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which sounds good but was actually bad for someone like me. I needed more rigid deadlines and check-ins. The freedom let me drift and procrastinate. More structure would have forced me to stay on track instead of falling behind.
Q19: The video content being available was great for my learning style. But the lack of frequent mandatory check-ins made it easy to disengage. If there had been more required participation throughout, I might have stayed more connected. The design assumed self-motivation I didn't have.
Q20: That's kind of me, honestly. I was logging in frequently, watching videos, clicking around—but when it came to actually producing work or studying for exams, I fell short. High activity can just mean someone is present without being productive. Browsing isn't the same as learning.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone studies efficiently offline with good materials, they don't need to be on the LMS constantly. They're focused, they get it done, they perform well. My problem was the opposite—lots of activity but scattered, unfocused, not translating to actual understanding.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click, how long you spend. But they can't show if you're actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss offline study completely, and they can't capture the quality of engagement, just the quantity of clicks.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in focused effort. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's wrestling with concepts, asking questions, connecting ideas. I was present a lot but genuinely engaged? Not as much as the numbers might suggest.
Q24: The fear I've been carrying around wouldn't show up in any data. The CBT sessions I've been doing, the anxiety about failing, the way I'd avoid the course because facing it felt scary—none of that appears in login stats. My emotional state shaped everything but left no digital trace.
